questions.title: Question 1
questions.question: Which Auto Loader configuration will automatically detect and add new columns to the target Delta table?

choices.A: `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes = true`
choices.B: `cloudFiles.format = json` with `rescuedDataColumn`
choices.C: `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` with `mergeSchema = true`
choices.D: `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = addNewColumns`

questions.shortAnswer: `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = addNewColumns` is the Auto Loader configuration that automatically detects and adds new columns to the target schema. Option A only controls type inference, not schema evolution. Option C uses `schemaLocation` for storing schema but `mergeSchema` is a Delta write option, not Auto Loader. Option B captures unparseable data but doesn't handle schema evolution.

questions.title: Question 2
questions.question: Which trigger configuration balances latency and file size for this workload?

choices.A: `trigger(availableNow=True)`
choices.B: `trigger(processingTime="1 second")`
choices.C: `trigger(processingTime="1 minute")`
choices.D: `trigger(once=True)`

questions.shortAnswer: A 1-minute processing time trigger allows sufficient data to accumulate for larger file writes while maintaining reasonable latency. Option A processes all available data at once (batch-like). Option B writes too frequently, creating small files. Option D is for one-time batch processing, not continuous streaming.

questions.title: Question 3
questions.question: Which approach correctly implements this SCD Type 2 pattern using Delta Lake?

choices.A: Use `MERGE INTO` with `WHEN MATCHED AND target.is_current = true THEN UPDATE` to close the old record, then `INSERT` the new record
choices.B: Use `MERGE INTO` with `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE` to update the existing record
choices.C: Use `INSERT OVERWRITE` to replace all records for the customer
choices.D: Use `DELETE` followed by `INSERT` to replace the customer record

questions.shortAnswer: SCD Type 2 requires closing the current record (setting end_date and is_current=false) and inserting a new record. The MERGE statement with a condition on `is_current = true` ensures only the active record is updated. Options B and D implement SCD Type 1 (overwrite). Option C would lose history.

questions.title: Question 4
questions.question: Which Auto Loader configuration achieves both requirements?

choices.A: Set `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` and `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = rescue`
choices.B: Set `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes = true` and `badRecordsPath`
choices.C: Set `cloudFiles.schemaHints` with all expected columns
choices.D: Set `cloudFiles.format = json` with `mode = PERMISSIVE`

questions.shortAnswer: `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` persists the inferred schema to a directory so it's reused across restarts. `schemaEvolutionMode = rescue` captures data that doesn't match the schema into a `_rescued_data` column instead of failing. Option B's `badRecordsPath` is for batch reads. Option C provides hints but doesn't persist schema or rescue data. Option D uses invalid configuration syntax.

questions.title: Question 5
questions.question: Which combination ensures exactly-once processing guarantees?

choices.A: `outputMode("append")` with checkpointing enabled
choices.B: `outputMode("complete")` with `foreachBatch()` and manual deduplication
choices.C: `outputMode("append")` with checkpointing and idempotent Delta writes
choices.D: `outputMode("update")` with watermarking

questions.shortAnswer: Exactly-once semantics require checkpointing (for tracking progress) combined with idempotent writes (Delta Lake's transaction log ensures writes are atomic and can be retried safely). Option A lacks idempotency mention. Option B requires manual handling. Option D is for stateful aggregations.

questions.title: Question 6
questions.question: Which code correctly reads incremental changes from the CDF-enabled table?

choices.A: `spark.read.format("delta").option("readChangeFeed", "true").table("bronze")`
choices.B: `spark.readStream.format("delta").option("ignoreChanges", "true").table("bronze")`
choices.C: `spark.read.format("delta").option("startingVersion", last_version).table("bronze")`
choices.D: `spark.readStream.format("delta").option("readChangeFeed", "true").table("bronze")`

questions.shortAnswer: Using `readStream` with `readChangeFeed = true` creates a streaming source that automatically tracks progress and reads only new changes. Option A is batch read (would need version parameters). Option C reads snapshots, not change records. Option B ignores changes rather than reading them.

questions.title: Question 7
questions.question: What is the most effective optimization for this MERGE operation?

choices.A: Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` to 1000
choices.B: Add a Z-ORDER on `customer_id`
choices.C: Partition the table by `customer_id`
choices.D: Enable `spark.databricks.delta.merge.enableLowShuffle`

questions.shortAnswer: Z-ORDER on `customer_id` co-locates data with similar customer_id values, enabling file pruning during MERGE and dramatically reducing files scanned. Option A adds parallelism but doesn't reduce data scanned. Option C is impractical for high-cardinality columns. Option D is not a valid configuration.

questions.title: Question 8
questions.question: What is the expected behavior and how should the engineer address this?

choices.A: Increase the watermark to 20 minutes to capture more late data
choices.B: This is expected behavior; events beyond the watermark are intentionally dropped
choices.C: Set `spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.providerClass` to handle late data
choices.D: Use `outputMode("complete")` to include all data

questions.shortAnswer: Watermarking intentionally drops data arriving after the threshold to bound state store size. Events 15 minutes late with a 10-minute watermark will be dropped by design. Option A could work but increases state size. Options C and D don't address late data handling. The engineer should evaluate if 10 minutes is the right threshold for their use case.

questions.title: Question 9
questions.question: Which approach correctly handles multiple topics with independent schemas?

choices.A: Read all topics in one stream and use `filter()` to split by topic before writing
choices.B: Use `foreachBatch()` to route records to different tables based on topic
choices.C: Create separate streaming queries for each topic with independent checkpoints
choices.D: Use `readStream.format("kafka").option("subscribe", "topic1,topic2")` with schema registry

questions.shortAnswer: Separate streaming queries with independent checkpoints provide isolation for different schemas and independent failure handling. Option A requires a common schema. Option B complicates checkpoint management. Option D subscribes to multiple topics but doesn't handle different schemas well.

questions.title: Question 10
questions.question: What must be done to reclaim storage space after DELETE operations?

choices.A: Run `OPTIMIZE` to compact files
choices.B: Recreate the table using `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT`
choices.C: Run `FSCK REPAIR TABLE` to fix storage
choices.D: Run `VACUUM` with an appropriate retention period

questions.shortAnswer: DELETE in Delta Lake marks records as removed but doesn't physically delete files (to support time travel). VACUUM removes files older than the retention period that are no longer referenced. Option A compacts but doesn't remove old files. Option C checks table integrity. Option B is inefficient.

questions.title: Question 11
questions.question: Which configuration ensures accurate aggregations while bounding state store growth?

choices.A: Set `outputMode("complete")` to recompute all windows
choices.B: Add `withWatermark("timestamp", "2 hours")` before the aggregation
choices.C: Use `outputMode("append")` without watermarking
choices.D: Set `spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.maintenanceInterval` to "1 hour"

questions.shortAnswer: Watermarking allows Spark to handle late data within the threshold (2 hours) while cleaning up state for windows that can no longer receive updates. Option A recomputes but doesn't bound state. Option C would drop late data immediately. Option D controls maintenance frequency, not late data handling.

questions.title: Question 12
questions.question: What is the most likely cause of this issue?

choices.A: The checkpoint location is full
choices.B: Auto Loader's `cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger` is set too low
choices.C: The source files are being written to a different path than configured
choices.D: The stream is waiting for the trigger interval

questions.shortAnswer: A stream showing ACTIVE with 0 rows processed while files are arriving typically indicates the configured source path doesn't match where files are being written. Option A would cause errors, not zero processing. Option B would limit throughput but not stop it entirely. Option D wouldn't show persistent zero processing.

questions.title: Question 13
questions.question: Which approach correctly handles this deduplication requirement?

choices.A: Use `dropDuplicates()` on the source DataFrame before MERGE
choices.B: Use multiple MERGE statements with different conditions
choices.C: Set `spark.databricks.delta.merge.repartitionBeforeWrite.enabled = true`
choices.D: Deduplicate within the MERGE using `ROW_NUMBER()` in a subquery

questions.shortAnswer: Using `ROW_NUMBER()` partitioned by key and ordered by timestamp DESC in a subquery, then filtering for row_number = 1, deduplicates source data correctly within the MERGE while ensuring the latest record is kept. Option A (`dropDuplicates()`) is non-deterministic and doesn't guarantee keeping the record with the latest timestamp. Option C affects write performance, not deduplication. Option B is unnecessarily complex.

questions.title: Question 14
questions.question: Which configuration captures malformed records while allowing the pipeline to continue?

choices.A: Set `cloudFiles.ignoreCorruptFiles = true`
choices.B: Set `badRecordsPath` to a storage location
choices.C: Use `cloudFiles.rescuedDataColumn` to capture unparseable data
choices.D: Wrap the read in a try-catch block

questions.shortAnswer: `rescuedDataColumn` creates a column containing data that couldn't be parsed according to the schema, allowing analysis of bad records. Option A ignores corrupt files entirely. Option B is for batch operations. Option D doesn't work with streaming transformations.

questions.title: Question 15
questions.question: Which code pattern correctly implements incremental processing using CDF?

choices.A: Read with `option("readChangeFeed", "true").option("startingVersion", last_processed_version)`
choices.B: Read with `option("versionAsOf", last_processed_version)` and compare with current version
choices.C: Use `CHANGES` table-valued function in SQL
choices.D: Read the `_delta_log` directly to find changed files

questions.shortAnswer: Reading CDF with `startingVersion` returns all changes (inserts, updates, deletes) since that version, including `_change_type` column. Option B reads snapshots, not changes. Option C is not valid syntax. Option D is fragile and not recommended.

questions.title: Question 16
questions.question: How does Delta Lake ensure consistent reads during concurrent writes?

choices.A: Delta uses table-level locks to prevent reads during writes
choices.B: Delta uses snapshot isolation; readers see consistent snapshots regardless of concurrent writes
choices.C: Delta queues read requests until writes complete
choices.D: Configure `delta.isolationLevel = serializable` to ensure consistency

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Lake provides snapshot isolation by default. Readers see a consistent snapshot based on the transaction log at query start time, regardless of concurrent writes. Option A is incorrect; Delta doesn't use table locks. Option C is incorrect; reads aren't queued. Option D is not a valid setting.

questions.title: Question 17
questions.question: Which MERGE clause correctly implements this conditional update logic?

choices.A: `WHEN MATCHED AND source.timestamp > target.timestamp THEN UPDATE SET *`
choices.B: `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHERE source.timestamp > target.timestamp`
choices.C: `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.* = source.* IF source.timestamp > target.timestamp`
choices.D: `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * HAVING source.timestamp > target.timestamp`

questions.shortAnswer: The condition in `WHEN MATCHED AND condition` filters which matched rows receive the update. Rows not meeting the condition are skipped. Option B has invalid syntax (WHERE after UPDATE SET). Options C and D use invalid syntax.

questions.title: Question 18
questions.question: How can the engineer achieve atomic writes across multiple tables in `foreachBatch()`?

choices.A: Wrap all writes in a single Spark transaction using `spark.sql("BEGIN TRANSACTION")`
choices.B: Use Delta Lake's multi-table transactions with `spark.databricks.delta.multiTableTransaction.enabled`
choices.C: This cannot be achieved directly; implement idempotent writes with manual rollback logic
choices.D: Use `foreachPartition()` instead for transactional guarantees

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Lake doesn't support multi-table transactions. The recommended approach is to make each write idempotent (using merge keys or batch IDs) so failed batches can be safely reprocessed. Options A and B describe non-existent features. Option D doesn't provide multi-table atomicity.

questions.title: Question 19
questions.question: Which approach correctly implements parameterization for both use cases?

choices.A: Use `dbutils.widgets.text()` with defaults that can be overridden by job parameters
choices.B: Use environment variables set at cluster startup
choices.C: Use `spark.conf.get()` to read parameters from cluster configuration
choices.D: Hard-code values and create separate notebooks for each environment

questions.shortAnswer: `dbutils.widgets` creates parameters with defaults for interactive use that can be overridden when called from jobs using the notebook task parameters. Option B requires cluster configuration. Option C requires cluster-level settings. Option D is unmaintainable.

questions.title: Question 20
questions.question: Which approach correctly captures SQL results in a Python variable?

choices.A: Use `%sql` and reference the `_sqldf` variable
choices.B: Use `%sql` with `INTO :variable_name` syntax
choices.C: Use `spark.sql()` and assign to a variable
choices.D: Use `%run` to execute a SQL notebook and capture results

questions.shortAnswer: `spark.sql("SELECT ...")` returns a DataFrame that can be assigned to a variable for further processing in Python. This is the recommended programmatic approach. Option A's `_sqldf` does exist in Databricks notebooks (it captures the last %sql result) but is implicit and less reliable for production code. Option B has invalid syntax. Option D doesn't capture SQL results.

questions.title: Question 21
questions.question: Which approach correctly implements secure credential management?

choices.A: Store the token in a notebook cell and delete it after running
choices.B: Store the token in a Delta table with restricted access
choices.C: Use environment variables on the cluster
choices.D: Use `dbutils.secrets.get(scope="api_scope", key="api_token")`

questions.shortAnswer: Databricks Secrets provides secure credential storage with access control. Secrets are encrypted at rest and redacted in logs. Option A exposes secrets in notebook history. Option B is not designed for secrets. Option C requires cluster admin access and isn't as secure.

questions.title: Question 22
questions.question: Which approach provides centralized code sharing with automatic updates?

choices.A: Copy utility functions into each notebook
choices.B: Use `%run /Workspace/Shared/utilities` at the start of each notebook
choices.C: Package utilities as a wheel file and install via `%pip install`
choices.D: Store utilities in a Delta table and read them at runtime

questions.shortAnswer: `%run` executes another notebook in the current notebook's context, making all its functions available. Changes to the shared notebook are immediately available to all callers. Option A requires manual updates. Option C requires reinstallation for updates. Option D is impractical for code.

questions.title: Question 23
questions.question: Which authentication method is most appropriate for this CI/CD scenario?

choices.A: OAuth user-to-machine authentication
choices.B: Personal access token stored in `DATABRICKS_TOKEN` environment variable
choices.C: Service principal with `DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID` and `DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET`
choices.D: Username and password authentication

questions.shortAnswer: Service principals with OAuth are the recommended approach for CI/CD automation. They provide machine identity without personal credentials and can be scoped with specific permissions. Option A requires user interaction. Option B uses personal tokens that expire. Option D is deprecated.

questions.title: Question 24
questions.question: What is the most likely cause and solution?

choices.A: The cluster creation is asynchronous; poll the cluster status until it's RUNNING
choices.B: The API token doesn't have permission to access the cluster
choices.C: The cluster_id was not properly formatted in the job request
choices.D: Rate limiting is preventing the second API call

questions.shortAnswer: Cluster creation via API returns immediately with a cluster_id, but the cluster takes time to provision. The job API call failed because the cluster wasn't ready yet. The solution is to poll `GET /api/2.0/clusters/get` until state is RUNNING. Options B-D are possible but less likely given the described sequence.

questions.title: Question 25
questions.question: Which recommendation optimizes for cost while maintaining reliability?

choices.A: Use all-purpose clusters with auto-termination to save costs
choices.B: Use a shared all-purpose cluster across all jobs to maximize utilization
choices.C: Use serverless compute for all jobs regardless of workload characteristics
choices.D: Use job clusters which are automatically created and terminated per job run

questions.shortAnswer: Job clusters are created for each job run and terminated afterward, providing cost savings (lower DBU rates) and isolation between runs. Option A still has higher DBU rates. Option B risks resource contention and failures affecting multiple jobs. Option C may not be cost-effective for predictable scheduled workloads.

questions.title: Question 26
questions.question: Which approach correctly accesses Unity Catalog volumes?

choices.A: `dbutils.fs.ls("dbfs:/Volumes/catalog/schema/volume")`
choices.B: `dbutils.fs.ls("/Volumes/catalog/schema/volume")`
choices.C: `spark.read.format("binaryFile").load("dbfs:/Volumes/catalog/schema/volume")`
choices.D: `os.listdir("/dbfs/Volumes/catalog/schema/volume")`

questions.shortAnswer: Unity Catalog volumes are accessed via the `/Volumes` path without the `dbfs:` prefix. Option A uses the wrong path format. Option C reads file content, not metadata. Option D may work but `dbutils.fs` is the recommended approach for Databricks.

questions.title: Question 27
questions.question: How should the job task be configured to pass the parameter?

choices.A: In the notebook task configuration, set `base_parameters: {"env": "prod"}`
choices.B: Set cluster environment variable `ENV=prod`
choices.C: Use `spark.conf.set("env", "prod")` in the job configuration
choices.D: Create a separate notebook for production without widgets

questions.shortAnswer: The notebook task's `base_parameters` map directly to widget values, overriding defaults when the notebook runs as a job. Option B doesn't connect to widgets. Option C sets Spark config, not widget values. Option D is unmaintainable.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.question: Which API endpoint and request body correctly submits this job run?

choices.A: POST `/api/2.1/jobs/create` with `notebook_params` in the request
choices.B: PUT `/api/2.1/jobs/update` with `notebook_params` in the settings
choices.C: POST `/api/2.1/jobs/runs/submit` with `notebook_params` in the task configuration
choices.D: POST `/api/2.1/jobs/run-now` with `notebook_params` in the request

questions.shortAnswer: `run-now` triggers an existing job with parameter overrides via `notebook_params`. Option A creates a job definition but doesn't run it. Option C submits a one-time run (requires full job spec). Option B updates job definition, doesn't run it.

questions.title: Question 29
questions.question: What is the recommended migration approach for accessing cloud storage with Unity Catalog?

choices.A: Update all paths to use `abfss://` or `s3://` URLs directly
choices.B: Create external locations in Unity Catalog and use `/Volumes/` paths
choices.C: Keep using mounts but register them as external locations
choices.D: Use storage credentials to access cloud storage via `dbutils.fs`

questions.shortAnswer: Unity Catalog volumes provide governed access to cloud storage with paths like `/Volumes/catalog/schema/volume/`. External locations and volumes replace the ungoverned mount pattern. Option A bypasses governance. Option C mixes patterns incorrectly. Option D doesn't leverage Unity Catalog.

questions.title: Question 30
questions.question: Which CLI command correctly executes a SQL query and formats output as JSON?

choices.A: `databricks sql execute --query "SELECT * FROM table" --format JSON`
choices.B: `databricks workspace execute-sql --statement "SELECT * FROM table" --output json`
choices.C: `databricks sql-cli -e "SELECT * FROM table" -o json`
choices.D: `databricks api post /api/2.0/sql/statements -d '{"statement": "SELECT * FROM table"}'`

questions.shortAnswer: The Databricks CLI can make arbitrary API calls. The SQL Statement Execution API (`/api/2.0/sql/statements`) executes SQL queries programmatically. Options A-C use non-existent CLI commands. The actual implementation may vary by CLI version, but the API approach always works.

questions.title: Question 31
questions.question: Which layer should handle flattening the nested JSON structure into a relational format?

choices.A: Bronze layer - ingest and flatten immediately
choices.B: Gold layer - create denormalized tables for analytics
choices.C: Silver layer - transform raw data into cleaned, conformed structures
choices.D: A separate staging layer before bronze

questions.shortAnswer: The silver layer is responsible for data cleansing and conforming, including flattening nested structures. Bronze should preserve raw data as-is for auditability. Gold focuses on business aggregations. Option D adds unnecessary complexity.

questions.title: Question 32
questions.question: What happens when this data is written to the table?

choices.A: Null values are converted to empty strings
choices.B: The write fails with a constraint violation error
choices.C: Records with null emails are silently dropped
choices.D: The NOT NULL constraint is automatically removed

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Lake enforces NOT NULL constraints at write time. Attempting to write null values to a NOT NULL column causes the entire write operation to fail with an error. Options A, C, and D describe behaviors that don't occur.

questions.title: Question 33
questions.question: Which statement correctly adds this column?

choices.A: `ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier STRING AFTER customer_id`
choices.B: `ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMNS (loyalty_tier STRING AFTER customer_id)`
choices.C: `ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier STRING` (column position cannot be specified)
choices.D: Table must be recreated to add a column at a specific position

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Lake supports column positioning with `FIRST` or `AFTER` clauses in ADD COLUMN statements. The syntax `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN column_name TYPE AFTER existing_column` correctly positions the new column. Option B uses incorrect syntax (COLUMNS instead of COLUMN with positioning). Option C is incorrect because position CAN be specified. Option D is unnecessary since ADD COLUMN with AFTER is supported.

questions.title: Question 34
questions.question: How many rows should be affected by this single address change?

choices.A: 1 row (update the existing record)
choices.B: 2 rows (close old record, insert new record)
choices.C: 3 rows (update old, insert new, update audit table)
choices.D: Depends on the number of historical records for that customer

questions.shortAnswer: SCD Type 2 handles changes by: (1) closing the current record (setting end_date and is_current=false) and (2) inserting a new record with the new address (is_current=true). This affects exactly 2 rows. Option A describes SCD Type 1. Options C and D describe non-standard implementations.

questions.title: Question 35
questions.question: What modification would most improve query performance?

choices.A: Add more partitions by also partitioning on `customer_id`
choices.B: Remove date partitioning and use Z-ORDER on `customer_id, product_id`
choices.C: Keep date partitioning and add Z-ORDER on `customer_id, product_id`
choices.D: Convert to Liquid Clustering on `customer_id, product_id`

questions.shortAnswer: Liquid Clustering on the frequently filtered columns provides automatic data organization and maintenance. Since queries rarely filter on date, date partitioning provides little benefit. Option B loses time-based data management benefits. Option C combines both but has more maintenance overhead than liquid clustering. Option A creates too many small partitions.

questions.title: Question 36
questions.question: Which Delta Lake feature and configuration supports this requirement?

choices.A: Enable Change Data Feed to track all changes
choices.B: Create a separate history table with trigger-based logging
choices.C: Time travel with default retention (30 days log retention)
choices.D: Use Delta Lake versioning with `delta.logRetentionDuration = 30 days`

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Lake's time travel allows querying historical versions using `VERSION AS OF` or `TIMESTAMP AS OF`. The default 30-day log retention supports this requirement. Option A tracks changes but doesn't provide point-in-time queries. Option B is unnecessary given Delta's built-in capabilities. Option D describes the feature partially but misses that it's the default behavior.

questions.title: Question 37
questions.question: What is the default behavior in Delta Lake and how can it be controlled?

choices.A: Delta is case-insensitive by default; both names map to the same column
choices.B: Delta is case-sensitive by default; they become separate columns
choices.C: Delta throws an error on case conflicts
choices.D: Delta automatically renames conflicting columns with suffixes

questions.shortAnswer: By default, Delta Lake (following Spark SQL) treats column names as case-insensitive. "CustomerId" and "customerid" refer to the same column. This behavior can be changed with `spark.sql.caseSensitive = true`. Options B, C, and D describe non-default behaviors.

questions.title: Question 38
questions.question: How do changes to the production table affect the cloned table?

choices.A: Changes automatically propagate to the clone
choices.B: Changes propagate until the clone is modified, then it becomes independent
choices.C: Only schema changes propagate; data changes don't
choices.D: The clone is completely independent; changes don't propagate

questions.shortAnswer: Deep clone creates a completely independent copy with its own data files. After cloning, both tables evolve independently. No changes propagate between them. Option A describes linked tables, not clones. Options B and C describe behaviors that don't exist.

questions.title: Question 39
questions.question: Which SCD type best supports this requirement?

choices.A: SCD Type 1 with a separate audit log table
choices.B: SCD Type 2 with effective dates
choices.C: SCD Type 3 with previous value columns
choices.D: SCD Type 6 (hybrid) with Type 1, 2, and 3 elements

questions.shortAnswer: For corrections (not legitimate changes), SCD Type 1 updates the current value while a separate audit log table preserves the history of corrections. Type 2 is for legitimate business changes, not error corrections. Type 3 only tracks one previous value. Type 6 is complex and not specifically designed for corrections.

questions.title: Question 40
questions.question: Which Unity Catalog permission model correctly implements this requirement?

choices.A: Grant SELECT on each table individually as they're created
choices.B: Create a view for each table and grant SELECT on views only
choices.C: Grant SELECT on the catalog to give access to all schemas and tables
choices.D: Grant USE SCHEMA and SELECT on SCHEMA to give access to all current and future tables

questions.shortAnswer: Granting SELECT at the schema level provides access to all current and future tables in that schema. USE SCHEMA is required to access objects within the schema. Option A requires ongoing maintenance. Option C is too broad. Option B is unnecessarily complex.

questions.title: Question 41
questions.question: Which Unity Catalog feature implements this column-level security requirement?

choices.A: Create separate tables for HR and managers with different columns
choices.B: Use dynamic views with `is_account_group_member()` to filter columns
choices.C: Use column masks to hide salary for non-HR users
choices.D: Implement application-level security in the BI tool

questions.shortAnswer: Column masks in Unity Catalog apply functions to column values based on the querying user's permissions, showing the real value to authorized users and masked/null values to others. Option A creates data duplication. Option B requires view maintenance. Option D doesn't protect data at the source.

questions.title: Question 42
questions.question: Which feature enables secure data sharing with external organizations?

choices.A: Create a service principal for the partner to access via API
choices.B: Use Delta Sharing to share data via open protocol
choices.C: Export data daily to a shared cloud storage location
choices.D: Create a Databricks workspace for the partner with access to the dataset

questions.shortAnswer: Delta Sharing provides an open protocol for secure data sharing across organizations and platforms. Partners can use compatible clients (including Snowflake, Spark, pandas) without Databricks access. Option A requires workspace access. Option C requires file transfer management. Option D is costly and requires Databricks licenses.

questions.title: Question 43
questions.question: Which code correctly retrieves the database password?

choices.A: `dbutils.secrets.get(scope="keyvault-scope", key="db-password")`
choices.B: `spark.conf.get("azure.keyvault.db-password")`
choices.C: `dbutils.credentials.get("keyvault-scope", "db-password")`
choices.D: `%keyvault get db-password`

questions.shortAnswer: When Azure Key Vault is configured as a secret scope backend, secrets are accessed through `dbutils.secrets.get()` using the scope name and key name. The Key Vault integration is transparent to the code. Options B, C, and D use invalid syntax.

questions.title: Question 44
questions.question: What permissions does the user have on that table in Workspace B?

choices.A: No permissions; grants are workspace-specific
choices.B: Same SELECT permission; Unity Catalog permissions are centralized
choices.C: Read-only permissions with a warning about cross-workspace access
choices.D: Permissions depend on workspace-level settings

questions.shortAnswer: Unity Catalog provides centralized governance across all workspaces attached to the same metastore. Permissions granted on objects apply regardless of which workspace is used to access them. This is a key benefit of Unity Catalog over legacy Hive metastore.

questions.title: Question 45
questions.question: Which approach correctly implements this row-level security in Unity Catalog?

choices.A: Add a row filter using `ROW FILTER filter_function ON (patient_id)`
choices.B: Create dynamic views with `WHERE doctor_id = current_user()`
choices.C: Use column masks to hide rows from unauthorized users
choices.D: Implement filtering in the application layer only

questions.shortAnswer: Unity Catalog row filters apply a function that determines which rows each user can access based on their identity. The filter function can join with assignment tables to implement complex rules. Option B works but requires view maintenance. Option C is for column security, not row security. Option D doesn't protect data at the source.

questions.title: Question 46
questions.question: Which system table provides this billing information?

choices.A: `system.compute.clusters`
choices.B: `system.access.audit`
choices.C: `system.billing.usage`
choices.D: `system.workflow.jobs`

questions.shortAnswer: The `system.billing.usage` table contains detailed DBU consumption data by workspace, cluster, and SKU. It's the primary source for cost analysis. Option A has cluster metadata. Option B has security audit logs. Option D has job definitions, not cost data.

questions.title: Question 47
questions.question: What does this pattern indicate and what is the recommended solution?

choices.A: Insufficient executors; increase cluster size
choices.B: Data skew; enable AQE skew join handling or salt the skewed key
choices.C: Memory pressure; increase executor memory
choices.D: Network bottleneck; check cluster networking

questions.shortAnswer: One slow task with idle executors is the classic sign of data skew--one partition has much more data than others. AQE can automatically split skewed partitions, or you can manually salt keys. Option A wouldn't help if executors are idle. Options C and D show different symptoms.

questions.title: Question 48
questions.question: Which approach retrieves the expectation violation details?

choices.A: Check the cluster driver logs
choices.B: Review the pipeline settings in the UI
choices.C: Use `dbutils.pipelines.getLatestUpdate()` API
choices.D: Query the pipeline's event log table for expectation metrics

questions.shortAnswer: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines stores detailed metrics including expectation results in the event log. Querying the event log with filters for the update ID shows which expectations failed and the row counts. Option A has limited detail. Option C isn't a valid API. Option B shows configuration, not runtime details.

questions.title: Question 49
questions.question: Which tool reveals whether partition pruning is occurring?

choices.A: Run `ANALYZE TABLE` to update statistics
choices.B: Check the Query History for execution time breakdown
choices.C: Run `EXPLAIN` and check for `PartitionFilters` in the plan
choices.D: Review the table's `DESCRIBE DETAIL` output

questions.shortAnswer: `EXPLAIN` shows the query execution plan including `PartitionFilters` (pruned partitions) and `PushedFilters` (filters pushed to scan). Empty `PartitionFilters` indicates no pruning occurred. Option A updates statistics but doesn't show the plan. Options B and D don't show execution details.

questions.title: Question 50
questions.question: Which system table contains this access information?

choices.A: `system.billing.usage`
choices.B: `system.access.audit`
choices.C: `system.information_schema.tables`
choices.D: `system.compute.clusters`

questions.shortAnswer: The `system.access.audit` table contains audit logs of actions including table reads, with user identity and timestamp. Filter by `action_name` and `request_params` for table access events. Option A has billing data. Option C has table metadata. Option D has cluster info.

questions.title: Question 51
questions.question: Which factors should be investigated first?

choices.A: Data volume growth and partition sizing
choices.B: Cluster hardware degradation
choices.C: Changes to Databricks Runtime
choices.D: Network latency to cloud storage

questions.shortAnswer: Gradual performance degradation with unchanged code typically indicates data volume growth or small file accumulation (affecting read performance). Check table sizes, file counts, and partition strategies. Option B is unlikely to be gradual. Options C and D would cause immediate changes, not gradual.

questions.title: Question 52
questions.question: Which `databricks.yml` structure correctly defines these environments?

choices.A: Create separate `databricks-dev.yml`, `databricks-staging.yml`, `databricks-prod.yml` files
choices.B: Use `environments:` section with runtime variable substitution
choices.C: Use `targets:` section with `dev:`, `staging:`, and `prod:` subsections
choices.D: Create separate bundles for each environment in different directories

questions.shortAnswer: DAB uses a `targets:` section to define environment-specific configurations. Each target can override bundle settings like workspace host, cluster configs, and variables. Option A doesn't support DAB's inheritance model. Option B uses invalid syntax. Option D duplicates code.

questions.title: Question 53
questions.question: Which testing approach is most appropriate?

choices.A: Deploy code to a Databricks cluster and run tests remotely
choices.B: Skip unit tests and rely on integration tests only
choices.C: Use Nutter framework with Databricks Connect
choices.D: Use local Spark session in pytest with mocked data

questions.shortAnswer: Local Spark sessions in pytest allow fast unit tests without cluster costs or dependencies. Mock DataFrames test transformation logic efficiently. Option A is slow and costly for unit tests. Option C still requires cluster resources. Option B misses the value of unit testing.

questions.title: Question 54
questions.question: What is the recommended workflow for making changes?

choices.A: Create a feature branch, commit changes, and create a pull request
choices.B: Disable branch protection temporarily to push changes
choices.C: Use `%git` magic commands to force push to main
choices.D: Make changes in a separate notebook outside Git folders

questions.shortAnswer: The standard Git workflow with branch protection: create a feature branch, make changes, push, create PR for review, then merge to main after approval. This ensures code review and testing. Options B and C bypass protections. Option D loses version control benefits.

questions.title: Question 55
questions.question: Which authentication method is recommended for GitHub Actions?

choices.A: Store personal access token in GitHub Secrets
choices.B: Configure OIDC federation between GitHub and Databricks with service principal
choices.C: Use username and password stored in GitHub Secrets
choices.D: Create a dedicated user account for GitHub Actions

questions.shortAnswer: OIDC federation with service principals is the recommended approach for CI/CD authentication. GitHub Actions can authenticate using short-lived tokens without storing long-lived credentials. Option A uses personal tokens that expire. Option C is deprecated. Option D creates management overhead.

questions.title: Question 56
questions.question: Which Nutter pattern correctly implements this test case?

choices.A: Create an empty table in `before_all()` and clean up in `after_all()`
choices.B: Use `assertion_empty_table()` with the expected result
choices.C: Mock the Delta table read using `unittest.mock`
choices.D: Use Nutter's built-in empty table generator

questions.shortAnswer: Nutter's `before_all()` runs setup before tests (create empty test table), and `after_all()` runs cleanup (drop table). The actual test verifies behavior with the empty table. Option B isn't a Nutter method. Option C is complex within Nutter. Option D doesn't exist.

questions.title: Question 57
questions.question: What is the correct approach to resolve this and complete the deployment?

choices.A: Manually delete the resource and redeploy
choices.B: Update the resource name in databricks.yml to avoid conflict
choices.C: Run `databricks bundle destroy` then `databricks bundle deploy`
choices.D: Run `databricks bundle deploy --force` to overwrite

questions.shortAnswer: The `--force` flag allows DAB to take ownership of and update existing resources that match the bundle definition. This handles partial deployments gracefully. Option A risks missing dependent resources. Option C destroys everything unnecessarily. Option B changes the deployment, not fixing it.

questions.title: Question 58
questions.question: What is the most likely cause?

choices.A: The pipeline is configured for triggered execution, not continuous
choices.B: Materialized views don't automatically refresh from streaming tables
choices.C: There's a data type mismatch between the tables
choices.D: The materialized view requires a manual REFRESH command

questions.shortAnswer: In triggered execution mode, materialized views only refresh when the pipeline runs. For near real-time updates, use continuous mode or schedule frequent pipeline runs. In continuous mode, materialized views refresh as streaming tables update. Options B and D are incorrect; Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines handles refreshes automatically within pipeline runs. Option C would cause errors, not stale data.

questions.title: Question 59
questions.question: How does APPLY CHANGES handle out-of-order records by default?

choices.A: Records are processed in arrival order, ignoring timestamps
choices.B: Records are processed by sequence column; out-of-order records are dropped
choices.C: Records are processed by sequence column; out-of-order records update if sequence is higher
choices.D: The pipeline fails on out-of-order detection

questions.shortAnswer: APPLY CHANGES uses the SEQUENCE BY column to determine record order. If a later-arriving record has a higher sequence value than the current record, it updates the target. Records with lower sequence values than existing records are ignored (as they represent older data). This handles out-of-order arrival correctly.

questions.title: Question 60
questions.question: How can the dropped row count be retrieved?

choices.A: Check the `dropped_records` column in the target table
choices.B: Dropped rows are not tracked; add manual logging
choices.C: Run `DESCRIBE HISTORY` on the target table
choices.D: Query the pipeline event log for expectation metrics

questions.shortAnswer: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines stores detailed expectation metrics in the event log including `num_dropped_records` for each expectation. Query the event log filtering for expectation events. Option A doesn't exist. Option C shows Delta operations, not expectation details. Option B is incorrect; Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines tracks this automatically.

questions.title: Question 61
questions.question: What is the optimal compaction strategy?

choices.A: Run `OPTIMIZE` to compact files to the default 1GB target size
choices.B: Run `VACUUM` to remove small files
choices.C: Enable auto-compaction for future writes and run `OPTIMIZE` for existing files
choices.D: Repartition the table with `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 2000`

questions.shortAnswer: `OPTIMIZE` compacts existing small files into larger ones (target ~1GB). Enabling auto-compaction (`spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled`) prevents small files in future writes. Option B removes old files, not compaction. Option D affects partitions in processing, not file sizes.

questions.title: Question 62
questions.question: Which optimization would most improve this join performance?

choices.A: Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` to 1000
choices.B: Enable broadcast join by setting `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold = 100MB`
choices.C: Z-ORDER the fact table by the join key
choices.D: Partition both tables by the join key

questions.shortAnswer: A 50MB dimension table is a good candidate for broadcast join (eliminates shuffle of the large fact table). Increasing the threshold from 10MB default to 100MB enables automatic broadcast. Option A doesn't reduce shuffle. Option C helps filters, not joins. Option D requires data redistribution.

questions.title: Question 63
questions.question: Which configuration change most likely resolves this issue?

choices.A: Configure Lakeflow Jobs notifications (email + webhook to PagerDuty) for failures AND query `system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline` for historical failure analytics
choices.B: Add a Slack webhook inside the job's last task that fires on success; absence of message indicates failure
choices.C: Set up a SQL Alert that polls `SHOW JOBS` every 5 minutes
choices.D: Rely on Databricks support to surface failures automatically

questions.shortAnswer: OOM during shuffle typically means individual partitions are too large. Increasing shuffle partitions creates smaller partitions that fit in memory. Option B reduces available memory. Option C helps with small partitions, not OOM. Option D can cause off-heap OOM errors.
